topic: A musician hears their song being performed by someone else
raw_output:
[story 1]

The worn vinyl of the old record player hissed, and then, the melody spilled out—my song. It was a raw, aching thing, the very essence of everything I’d poured into those three minutes of acoustic guitar and whispered lyrics. I was in the cramped back room of the coffee shop, nursing a lukewarm latte, convinced no one would ever grasp the nuance I’d laid down. Then, the sound shifted. It wasn't a cover band; the phrasing was too intimate, the vibrato on the high note utterly familiar. My jaw tightened. I leaned forward, my knuckles whitening on the table edge, trying to isolate the source, to prove it was a fluke, a mishearing. But the voice was undeniably mine, rendered with a different timbre, a different breath. I gripped the mug until the ceramic felt hot against my palm, a tight knot forming low in my stomach. I didn't move, just watched, a rigid posture broadcasting a silent demand for clarification that no one was offering.

[story 2]

I paced the narrow hallway backstage, the echo of the performance still vibrating in my bones. Every muscle felt coiled, ready to spring, yet my feet refused to move toward the door. The sound had been unmistakable—my composition, stripped bare and reassembled by someone else’s hands. A strange, hot flush crept up my neck, warring with a deep, resistant chill. *Impossible.* I stopped pacing, planting my feet firmly, and stared at the empty space where the sound had originated. My breath hitched, shallow and quick. I wanted to storm out, to demand an explanation, but a strange paralysis held me captive. I clenched my fists until the nails dug crescent moons into my palms, refusing to let the surprise crack the carefully constructed wall I’d built around my creative vulnerability. I would wait. I would observe.

[story 3]

The applause died down, leaving a ringing silence in its wake. I stood by the curtain, my hands shoved deep into my pockets, feeling the unfamiliar prickle of irritation mixed with a stubborn refusal to accept the reality unfolding before me. That melody—my melody—was being spun by a stranger. It felt like a physical blow, jarring and deeply unfair. I took a deliberate, slow breath, trying to smooth the sudden tremor in my hands. I forced my gaze toward the stage, my expression carefully blank, masking the frantic internal scramble. I wanted to confront the performer, to shake them until they acknowledged the theft of my artistic soul. But I didn't. I simply stood there, rigid, letting the sheer weight of the situation settle, a silent, unyielding monument to my insistence that this couldn't be true.
